bytes.topic: Pipeline Design — lint → test → build → deploy stages, fail fast principle
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Pipeline Design — Fail Fast with lint → test → build → deploy
bytes.body:
Structure your GitHub Actions workflows so expensive jobs never run when cheap ones fail. Use `needs` to enforce strict job ordering and set `fail-fast: true` on matrix strategies to cancel sibling jobs immediately on failure.

Lint runs first — it's the cheapest gate. Use `actions/setup-node` with a cached `node_modules` and run ESLint/Prettier checks before any test runner spins up. If lint fails, the `test` job never queues. Tests follow: split unit and integration into separate jobs under `needs: [lint]` so they parallelize. The `build` job declares `needs: [test]` — it only runs once all test shards pass.

For deploy, gate on `needs: [build]` and add a condition like `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` so feature branches never touch production. Use `environment: production` to enforce required reviewers via GitHub's Environment Protection Rules — this is a native guardrail, not a script hack.

Upload build artifacts with `actions/upload-artifact` in the build job and download them in deploy — never rebuild in the deploy stage.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm
      - run: npm ci && npm run lint

  test:
    needs: [lint]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: true
      matrix:
        shard: [1, 2, 3]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci && npm test -- --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/3

  build:
    needs: [test]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/

  deploy:
    needs: [build]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
      - run: ./scripts/deploy.sh

bytes.topic: GitHub Actions — workflows, jobs, steps, reusable workflows, marketplace actions
bytes.title: GitHub Actions — Reusable Workflows vs Composite Actions: Pick the Right Abstraction
bytes.body: When DRYing up GitHub Actions pipelines, developers often reach for reusable workflows and composite actions interchangeably — but they serve different scopes. A reusable workflow (`workflow_call` trigger) runs as its own job graph, inheriting secrets via `secrets: inherit` and supporting matrix strategies. It's ideal for entire CI pipelines you want to share across repos. A composite action (`using: composite` in `action.yml`) bundles steps inside a single job, sharing the runner context, environment variables, and workspace with the calling job — ideal for step-level reuse like setup, linting, or artifact upload. Key gotcha: composite actions can't use `needs:` or define their own job-level `permissions`. Reusable workflows can, but they can't directly access the caller's `env` context — only `inputs` and `secrets`. For monorepos, composite actions win on speed (no extra job spin-up). For cross-repo standardization of full pipelines, reusable workflows are the right call. Mix both: a reusable workflow that internally calls composite actions for its steps.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml — calling a reusable workflow
jobs:
  test:
    uses: my-org/.github/.github/workflows/run-tests.yml@main
    with:
      node-version: '20'
    secrets: inherit

  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      # composite action for step-level reuse
      - uses: my-org/.github/actions/setup-node@main
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - run: npm run lint

bytes.topic: GitLab CI — stages, artifacts, cache, runners, include templates
bytes.title: GitHub Actions vs GitLab CI — Mapping Stages, Artifacts, Cache, Runners, and Templates
bytes.body:
GitLab CI's pipeline model maps cleanly to GitHub Actions, but the abstractions differ. Where GitLab uses `stages:` to enforce job ordering, GitHub Actions uses `needs:` per job — expressing an explicit dependency graph (DAG) rather than a flat stage list.

Artifacts become `actions/upload-artifact@v4` and `actions/download-artifact@v4`. Reference them by `name` across jobs; they're automatically scoped to the workflow run. Caching uses `actions/cache@v4` with a `key` + `restore-keys` fallback chain, mirroring GitLab's `cache.key` + `cache.policy`.

For runners, self-hosted GitHub runners replace GitLab's custom runners — tagged via `runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, x64]` instead of GitLab's `tags:`. GitHub-hosted runners (`ubuntu-latest`, `macos-latest`) have no direct GitLab equivalent.

GitLab's `include:` templates become reusable workflows (triggered via `workflow_call`) or composite actions. A reusable workflow lives in `.github/workflows/` and is invoked with `uses: org/repo/.github/workflows/shared.yml@main`, accepting typed `inputs` via `with:`. This is the idiomatic way to share pipeline logic across repositories without copy-pasting YAML.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.npm
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with: { name: dist, path: dist/ }
  deploy:
    needs: build  # replaces GitLab stages ordering
    runs-on: [self-hosted, linux]  # replaces GitLab runner tags
    steps:
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with: { name: dist }

bytes.topic: ArgoCD — GitOps model, sync policies, app of apps pattern
bytes.title: GitHub Actions + ArgoCD — GitOps Sync Policies and the App of Apps Pattern
bytes.body:
In a GitOps workflow, GitHub Actions owns the write side: it builds your image, bumps the tag in your config repo, and commits — ArgoCD owns the read side, continuously reconciling cluster state with that repo. The critical detail is the separation: your app repo triggers the Actions workflow; the workflow writes to a separate config repo; ArgoCD watches only the config repo.

Sync policies control how ArgoCD reacts to those commits. `automated.selfHeal: true` re-syncs if someone manually patches the cluster. `automated.prune: true` deletes resources removed from Git — dangerous without testing. Leave prune off in staging until your manifests are clean.

The app of apps pattern solves bootstrapping at scale. A root Application points to a directory of Application manifests. Your GitHub Actions workflow updates child app image tags; the root app syncs first and creates/updates children automatically. One ArgoCD install governs every environment. Use a matrix strategy in Actions — one job per environment — each committing to an environment-scoped path (`envs/prod/`, `envs/staging/`) that a dedicated child Application watches.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
jobs:
  gitops-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          repository: myorg/config-repo
          token: ${{ secrets.CONFIG_REPO_PAT }}
          path: config

      - name: Bump image tag
        run: |
          sed -i "s|tag: .*|tag: ${{ github.sha }}|" \
            config/envs/${{ matrix.env }}/values.yaml

      - name: Commit and push
        run: |
          cd config
          git config user.email "ci@myorg.com"
          git config user.name "GitHub Actions"
          git commit -am "chore: bump ${{ matrix.env }} to ${{ github.sha }}"
          git push

bytes.topic: Jenkins — pipeline as code, Jenkinsfile, shared libraries, agent config
bytes.title: GitHub Actions vs. Jenkins — Translating Jenkinsfiles, Shared Libraries, and Agent Config
bytes.body:
If you're inheriting a Jenkins pipeline or migrating one, here's how the mental model maps to GitHub Actions. A Jenkinsfile is Jenkins' workflow-as-code — its direct equivalent is `.github/workflows/*.yml`. Both live in-repo and define CI/CD declaratively, but Jenkinsfile uses a Groovy DSL while GitHub Actions uses YAML.

Jenkins' `agent { docker { image 'node:20' } }` maps to a `container: node:20` key under a job definition. For bare metal, Jenkins agents register via JNLP or SSH; GitHub self-hosted runners register with a token and are referenced by label: `runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, x64]`.

Shared libraries (`@Library('my-lib')`) centralize reusable pipeline logic across teams. GitHub Actions' equivalent is reusable workflows (`workflow_call` trigger) or composite actions stored in a separate repo and pinned via `uses: org/lib/.github/workflows/deploy.yml@v2`. Unlike Jenkins shared libraries — which are Groovy classes with full imperative logic — GitHub reusable workflows are pure YAML with typed `inputs`.

Jenkins' `parallel {}` stages translate to sibling jobs or `matrix:` strategies. Critical difference: GitHub Actions jobs don't share a workspace — pass data between jobs with `actions/upload-artifact` and `actions/download-artifact`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Jenkins: agent { docker { image 'node:20' } } + @Library('shared-lib')
# GitHub Actions equivalent:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    container:
      image: node:20
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      # Jenkins shared library → composite action from separate repo
      - uses: org/shared-actions/.github/actions/setup-node@v2
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - run: npm ci && npm test
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/

bytes.topic: Secrets in CI — never hardcode, vault integration, environment secrets
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Secrets — Vault Integration, Env Scopes, and What Never to Hardcode
bytes.body: GitHub Actions exposes secrets via `${{ secrets.MY_SECRET }}` — never via env vars you define inline. Hardcoding tokens in workflow YAML is a critical mistake: they appear in PR diffs, fork runs, and `actions/checkout` history. Use repository, environment, or organization-level secrets depending on blast radius. Environment secrets (Settings → Environments) gate values behind deployment protection rules — ideal for production credentials. For dynamic secrets or short-lived tokens, integrate HashiCorp Vault using `hashicorp/vault-action`: it authenticates via GitHub's OIDC token (`id-token: write` permission required), fetches secrets at runtime, and never stores them in your repo. For AWS, skip static IAM keys entirely — use `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` with OIDC to assume a role. Secrets are masked in logs automatically, but structured output (JSON, base64) can leak them — always pipe sensitive output through `jq` or mask manually with `echo "::add-mask::$VALUE"`. Never pass secrets as positional CLI args; they appear in `ps` output. Use environment variables or stdin instead.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3
        with:
          url: ${{ secrets.VAULT_ADDR }}
          method: jwt
          role: github-actions-role
          secrets: |
            secret/data/prod db_password | DB_PASSWORD ;
            secret/data/prod api_key    | API_KEY
      - name: mask dynamic value
        run: echo "::add-mask::$DB_PASSWORD"

bytes.topic: Caching — dependency caching, Docker layer caching, cache invalidation
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Caching — Dependency, Docker Layer, and Invalidation Strategies
bytes.body:
GitHub Actions' `actions/cache` stores artifacts between runs using a cache key. For Node.js, hash your lockfile: `key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}`. A miss falls back to `restore-keys` prefixes, giving you a warm partial cache. For Python, hash `requirements.txt`; for Gradle, hash `**/*.gradle*`.

Docker layer caching uses `cache-from` and `cache-to` with the `gha` backend via `docker/build-push-action`. Set `cache-to: type=gha,mode=max` to push all layers, not just the final image's. This cuts build times dramatically when your `COPY package.json` and `RUN npm install` layers stay unchanged.

Invalidation is key-driven — bump a suffix like `cache-v2-...` to bust stale caches. GitHub auto-evicts entries unused for 7 days or when the branch's total exceeds 10 GB. Scope caches to `github.ref` for branch isolation, or use `github.event.repository.default_branch` as a fallback restore key so PRs seed from main's warm cache.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Cache node_modules
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.npm
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
    restore-keys: |
      ${{ runner.os }}-node-

- name: Build and push Docker image
  uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
  with:
    push: true
    tags: ghcr.io/org/app:latest
    cache-from: type=gha
    cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

bytes.topic: Parallelization — matrix builds, parallel jobs, test sharding
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Parallelization — Matrix Builds, Parallel Jobs, and Test Sharding
bytes.body: GitHub Actions runs jobs concurrently by default when they have no `needs` dependency. Combine this with `strategy.matrix` to fan out builds across OS, runtime version, or any arbitrary dimension — each combination spawns an independent runner. For test sharding, pair matrix with `--shard` flags (Jest, Vitest, Playwright all support it): split your suite into N slices and run each slice on its own runner, cutting wall-clock time proportionally. Use `strategy.fail-fast: false` so a failing shard doesn't cancel sibling runners prematurely — you want the full failure picture. Cap concurrency with `strategy.max-parallel` to stay within your runner quota. For reusable parallelism patterns, extract logic into a reusable workflow called via `workflow_call` and invoke it with a matrix from the parent. One sharp edge: `upload-artifact` calls from parallel jobs must use unique `name` values or they'll collide. Use `${{ matrix.shard }}` in the artifact name. On large monorepos, combine this with `paths` filters on `on.push` to only trigger the relevant job matrix.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      max-parallel: 4
      matrix:
        shard: [1, 2, 3, 4]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npx vitest run --reporter=junit --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/${{ strategy.job-total }}
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: test-results-shard-${{ matrix.shard }}
          path: junit.xml

bytes.topic: Build Artifacts — versioning, storing, promoting across environments
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Build Artifacts — Version, Store, and Promote Without Rebuilding
bytes.body:
GitHub Actions artifacts are the promotion primitive between your build and deploy stages. Use `actions/upload-artifact@v4` with a version string built from `github.run_number` and the short SHA — this gives you sortable, traceable names with no external registry.

Pass the version downstream via `jobs.<id>.outputs` so every subsequent job downloads the exact same artifact. Never rebuild in a deploy job; download and ship. The `needs.<job>.outputs.version` pattern creates an auditable promotion chain: build → staging → prod, one artifact, zero rebuilds.

Control retention by branch: 90 days for `main`, 7 for pull requests. The `workflow_run` trigger lets a separate release workflow download artifacts from a completed CI workflow without coupling the two pipelines.

In v4, `merge-multiple: true` on `download-artifact` flattens outputs from matrix builds into one directory — critical when you're packaging multi-arch binaries. Combine this with a `needs` fan-in job that assembles and re-uploads a single versioned archive before promotion begins.
bytes.code_snippet:
build:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  outputs:
    version: ${{ steps.ver.outputs.tag }}
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - id: ver
      run: echo "tag=${{ github.run_number }}-${GITHUB_SHA::7}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
    - run: npm ci && npm run build
    - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
      with:
        name: app-${{ steps.ver.outputs.tag }}
        path: dist/
deploy-staging:
  needs: build
  steps:
    - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
      with:
        name: app-${{ needs.build.outputs.version }}

bytes.topic: Environment Promotion — dev → staging → prod gates, approval workflows
bytes.title: GitHub Actions — Environment Promotion Gates with Approval Workflows
bytes.body:
GitHub Actions' `environment` key on jobs is the backbone of promotion pipelines. Each environment (dev, staging, prod) can have protection rules: required reviewers, wait timers, and branch restrictions — all configured in repo Settings → Environments.

When a job targets an environment with required reviewers, GitHub pauses execution and sends approval requests before the job runs. Reference the environment in your job and GitHub handles the gate automatically.

Chain environments across jobs using `needs` to enforce sequential promotion. The deploy-staging job won't start until deploy-dev succeeds, and deploy-prod waits on a manual approval tied to the `production` environment.

Use `environment.url` to surface deployment URLs in the Actions UI — reviewers see the staging URL before approving production. Combine with `github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` conditions to restrict prod deploys to trunk only.

Environment secrets (Settings → Environments → Secrets) scope credentials per tier — staging gets its own `DATABASE_URL`, prod gets its own. Jobs automatically receive the right secrets based on the targeted environment, no manual switching needed.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  deploy-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: staging
      url: https://staging.example.com
    steps:
      - run: ./deploy.sh staging

  deploy-prod:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://example.com
    steps:
      - run: ./deploy.sh production

bytes.topic: Rollback Strategy — automated rollback on failed deploy
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Rollback Strategy — Automated Redeploy on Failed Deploy
bytes.body: When a deployment job fails, GitHub Actions can automatically trigger a rollback by chaining jobs with `needs` and `if` conditions. The pattern: run your deploy job, then define a `rollback` job that only executes when the deploy job fails (`if: failure()`). Inside the rollback job, use the GitHub CLI (`gh`) or the REST API to fetch the last successful deployment SHA via `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/deployments` filtered by `environment` and `state=success`. Once you have the SHA, re-trigger your deploy workflow using `gh workflow run` with that SHA as an input. For zero-downtime rollbacks on container-based deploys, store the previous image tag as a GitHub Actions variable using `gh variable set LAST_GOOD_TAG` at the end of each successful deploy. The rollback job then reads `${{ vars.LAST_GOOD_TAG }}` directly. Combine this with `deployment_status` event listeners to sync rollback state back to GitHub Environments, keeping your deployment history accurate and audit-ready.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./scripts/deploy.sh
      - if: success()
        run: gh variable set LAST_GOOD_TAG --body "${{ github.sha }}"
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

  rollback:
    needs: deploy
    if: failure()
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          ref: ${{ vars.LAST_GOOD_TAG }}
      - run: ./scripts/deploy.sh
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

bytes.topic: Preview Environments — ephemeral environments per PR, teardown on merge
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Preview Environments — Ephemeral Deployments Per PR, Gone on Merge
bytes.body: Every pull request gets its own live environment using two GitHub Actions workflows: one triggered on `pull_request` (types: `[opened, synchronize, reopened]`) to deploy, and one on `pull_request` (types: `[closed]`) to tear down. The deploy workflow uses `github.event.pull_request.number` to namespace resources — e.g., `preview-pr-${{ github.event.number }}` — so environments never collide. Post-deployment, `actions/github-script` writes the preview URL back as a PR comment using `octokit.rest.issues.createComment`. On merge or close, the teardown workflow runs regardless of merge outcome by listening to the `closed` event type, which fires for both merged and abandoned PRs. Use GitHub Environments (`environment: preview-pr-${{ github.event.number }}`) with `auto-inactivate-deployments: true` so the Deployments tab stays clean. Store per-PR state (resource IDs, URLs) in environment-scoped secrets or output them to a GitHub Environment variable via `gh variable set`. Combine with `concurrency: group: pr-${{ github.event.number }}` to cancel in-flight deploys when a new push arrives mid-review.
bytes.code_snippet:
name: Preview Deploy
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, closed]

jobs:
  deploy:
    if: github.event.action != 'closed'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: preview-pr-${{ github.event.number }}
      url: https://pr-${{ github.event.number }}.preview.example.com
    concurrency:
      group: pr-${{ github.event.number }}
      cancel-in-progress: true
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Deploy preview
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh --env pr-${{ github.event.number }}
      - name: Comment URL
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `Preview: https://pr-${{ github.event.number }}.preview.example.com`
            })

  teardown:
    if: github.event.action == 'closed'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./scripts/destroy.sh --env pr-${{ github.event.number }}

bytes.topic: Security Scanning — SAST, dependency scanning, container scanning in pipeline
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Security Pipeline — SAST, Dependency & Container Scanning
bytes.body: Shift security left by layering three scanning jobs in a single GitHub Actions workflow. For SAST, CodeQL runs as a reusable action — `github/codeql-action/analyze@v3` — with language auto-detection and uploads results to the Security tab via SARIF. For dependency scanning, Dependabot handles automated PRs, but for blocking CI, use `actions/dependency-review-action@v4` on pull_request events; it diffs the dependency graph between base and head SHAs and fails on CVSS score thresholds you control via `fail-on-severity`. For container images, `anchore/scan-action@v4` wraps Grype under the hood — point it at your built image digest, set `fail-build: true`, and pipe the SARIF output to `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3` so findings surface in the same Security tab. Run all three as parallel jobs under a `security` workflow triggered on push to main and on every PR. Gate merges with a required status check on the `security` workflow to enforce zero-critical policy without slowing feature branches.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  codeql:
    uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
    with:
      languages: javascript, python

  dependency-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/dependency-review-action@v4
        with:
          fail-on-severity: high

  container-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: anchore/scan-action@v4
        with:
          image: ${{ env.IMAGE_DIGEST }}
          fail-build: true
          severity-cutoff: critical
      - uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        with:
          sarif_file: results.sarif

bytes.topic: GitOps — declarative deployments, git as single source of truth
bytes.title: GitHub Actions GitOps — Git as Your Deployment Truth
bytes.body:
GitOps treats your repository as the single source of truth for infrastructure and application state. With GitHub Actions, you enforce this by triggering deployments exclusively on pushes to protected branches — never via manual kubectl or ad-hoc scripts.

The pattern: a `deploy.yml` workflow fires on `push` to `main`, reads Kubernetes manifests from `./k8s/`, and applies them via `kubectl apply -f`. The cluster converges to whatever is committed. No drift, no undocumented hotfixes.

Crucial enforcement: lock your workflow with `environment: production` and set required reviewers in GitHub's Environment settings. This adds an approval gate before the `kubectl` step runs, giving you audit trails via Actions logs.

For image tag management, avoid `latest`. Instead, use `${{ github.sha }}` to tag images and patch the manifest inline with `kustomize edit set image`. Commit that back to the repo — now the deployed SHA is always traceable in git history.

Pair with `on: pull_request` to run `kubectl diff` as a dry-run check, surfacing cluster delta before merge. Your PR becomes a deployment preview.
bytes.code_snippet:
name: GitOps Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Patch image tag
        run: |
          cd k8s
          kustomize edit set image app=ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.sha }}
      - name: Apply manifests
        run: kubectl apply -k k8s/
        env:
          KUBECONFIG_DATA: ${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG }}
      - name: Commit updated manifest
        run: |
          git config user.email "ci@byteai.dev"
          git config user.name "GitHub Actions"
          git add k8s/kustomization.yaml
          git commit -m "chore: deploy ${{ github.sha }}" && git push || true

bytes.topic: Self-hosted Runners — when to use, maintenance overhead, security
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Self-Hosted Runners — When to Use Them and What You're Signing Up For
bytes.body:
GitHub-hosted runners give you a clean VM per job, but they cap out at 2-core/7GB RAM and have no access to your private network. Self-hosted runners close both gaps — use them when jobs need >7GB RAM, must reach a private VPC, require a GPU, or when per-minute costs on large matrices add up fast.

Register a runner with `./config.sh --url https://github.com/ORG/REPO --token TOKEN` and target it via `runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, x64]` (or custom labels). Runner groups let you restrict access per repo or org.

The maintenance tax is real: you own OS patches, runner binary upgrades (`./run.sh` auto-updates, but only when a job fires), and ephemeral cleanup. Persistent runners leak secrets across jobs — use `--ephemeral` flag or the `actions/runner-controller` (ARC) on Kubernetes to spin up a fresh pod per job.

For security, never register a self-hosted runner on a public repo. Malicious PRs can execute arbitrary code on your machine. Always scope runners to private repos or protected environments, and run as a low-privilege service account.
bytes.code_snippet:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, gpu]
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run GPU benchmark
        run: python train.py --device cuda
      - name: Cleanup workspace
        if: always()
        run: rm -rf $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/*

bytes.topic: Pipeline Performance — measuring and optimizing slow pipelines
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Pipeline Performance — Measure First, Then Cut
bytes.body:
GitHub Actions logs expose per-step timing natively — click any step header in the run UI to see wall-clock duration. For systematic tracking, hit the REST API: `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/runs/{run_id}/timing`, which returns billable minutes broken down by OS.

The highest-leverage optimizations:

**Cache dependencies aggressively.** `actions/cache@v4` with a content-addressed key (`${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}`) cuts `npm ci` from ~90s to ~8s on cache hit. Layer `restore-keys` from specific to general for partial hits.

**Skip irrelevant triggers.** `on.push.paths` prevents your full CI suite from firing on docs-only commits. Combine with `paths-ignore` for fine-grained control.

**Cancel stale runs.** `concurrency.cancel-in-progress: true` scoped to `${{ github.ref }}` kills queued runs the moment a newer push arrives — critical on feature branches with rapid iteration.

**Parallelize ruthlessly.** Split lint, unit tests, and integration tests into separate top-level jobs. Use `needs:` only for true data dependencies, not convenience grouping. Idle runner time is wasted money.
bytes.code_snippet:
on:
  push:
    paths: ['src/**', 'package-lock.json']
concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.npm
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-node-
      - run: npm ci && npm run build

bytes.topic: Monorepo CI — affected detection, selective runs, turborepo/nx integration
bytes.title: GitHub Actions Monorepo CI — Affected Detection with Turborepo and NX
bytes.body:
Running full CI on every push in a monorepo burns minutes and money. GitHub Actions has no native affected detection, but Turborepo and NX fill the gap by hashing inputs and comparing against a baseline ref.

With Turborepo, set `TURBO_TOKEN` and `TURBO_TEAM` secrets to enable Remote Caching. Add `--filter=[HEAD^1]` to `turbo run test build` — Turborepo diffs against the previous commit and skips unchanged packages. For PRs, use `--filter=[origin/main...HEAD]` to scope against the base branch.

NX offers `nx affected --base=origin/main --head=HEAD` with a `--target=test` flag. Pair it with the `nrwl/nx-set-shas@v4` action to automatically compute `NX_BASE` and `NX_HEAD` from PR context, including merge-base resolution.

Both tools integrate with GitHub Actions' `paths` filter for coarse-grained gating (skip CI entirely when only docs change), then apply fine-grained affected logic inside the job. Use `actions/cache@v4` to persist `.turbo` or `.nx/cache` between runs, or lean on their hosted remote caches for cross-machine hit rates above 80%.
bytes.code_snippet:
- uses: nrwl/nx-set-shas@v4
  id: setSHAs

- name: Run affected tests
  run: |
    npx nx affected \
      --target=test \
      --base=${{ steps.setSHAs.outputs.base }} \
      --head=${{ steps.setSHAs.outputs.head }} \
      --parallel=3
  env:
    NX_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NX_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN }}

bytes.topic: Notifications — failure alerts, status badges, incident escalation
bytes.title: GitHub Actions — Failure Alerts, Status Badges, and Incident Escalation
bytes.body: When a GitHub Actions workflow fails, silence is the enemy. Wire up `if: failure()` steps to push Slack or PagerDuty alerts directly from your workflow using `curl` against their APIs — no third-party Actions required. Use `github.run_url` from the `github` context to embed a deep link to the failed run inside every alert payload, so on-call engineers land directly on the logs. For status badges, GitHub generates them automatically at `https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}/actions/workflows/{workflow-file}.yml/badge.svg` — pin these to your README with a branch filter (`?branch=main`) to reflect production health at a glance. For multi-stage pipelines, use `needs` context and `result == 'failure'` conditions on a dedicated `notify` job that aggregates failures across matrix jobs before escalating, preventing duplicate pages. Combine `GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` with `>> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` to write structured Markdown incident summaries directly into the workflow run UI, giving responders context without leaving GitHub.
bytes.code_snippet:
notify-on-failure:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  needs: [build, test, deploy]
  if: failure()
  steps:
    - name: Send PagerDuty alert
      run: |
        curl -X POST https://events.pagerduty.com/v2/enqueue \
          -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
          -d '{
            "routing_key": "'${{ secrets.PD_ROUTING_KEY }}'",
            "event_action": "trigger",
            "payload": {
              "summary": "CI failure on ${{ github.ref_name }}: ${{ github.run_url }}",
              "severity": "critical",
              "source": "github-actions"
            }
          }'
